title: Resolution Professional’s Statutory Mandate Trumps Arbitral Award Execution: The NCLT Chandigarh Perspective on Asset Control During CIRP
description:
The NCLT Chandigarh, in Parvinder Singh (Resolution Professional) v. Neel-Rattan Enterprises Private Limited and Others, delivered a significant ruling on the enforceability of a pre-CIRP arbitral award during the moratorium period, particularly concerning a Resolution Professional's (RP) statutory duty to secure corporate assets. The case involved Aabha Industries Limited (Corporate Debtor), whose factory premises were occupied by respondents based on an arbitral award predating the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP).

The Resolution Professional, mandated by IBC Sections 18 and 25(2)(a)(b), sought possession of a mortgaged factory unit. The respondents asserted their possessory rights, citing an arbitral award dated June 26, 2020, which preceded the CIRP commencement on December 23, 2022. They contended the award was final and the NCLT lacked jurisdiction to invalidate it under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act.

The Tribunal upheld the RP's application, affirming its comprehensive and residuary jurisdiction under IBC Section 60(5) for all CIRP-connected matters. It clarified that its role was to assess the arbitral award's effect and enforceability within the IBC framework, not its intrinsic validity. Crucially, the NCLT unequivocally ruled that the execution of the arbitral award was prohibited by the moratorium under IBC Section 14(1)(a). This provision expressly bars initiating or continuing proceedings, including execution of arbitral orders, against the Corporate Debtor during CIRP, irrespective of chronological priority, emphasizing the paramount objective of asset preservation and maximization.

Consequently, the NCLT directed the respondents to immediately hand over peaceful possession of the factory premises and all assets to the Resolution Professional within two weeks, authorizing police assistance for enforcement if required.

This judgment reinforces the hierarchical supremacy of insolvency legislation over private dispute resolution mechanisms when corporate assets are implicated. It establishes that an RP's statutory mandate to secure and preserve assets prevails over pre-existing arbitral rights, and the NCLT's Section 60(5) jurisdiction extends to adjudicating possessory disputes affecting CIRP, thereby insulating the collective resolution process from individual creditor actions and upholding the Code's objective of maximizing value for all stakeholders.

title: RBI Overhauls the Authorised Person Framework: A Close Reading of the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026
description:
The Reserve Bank of India has introduced the Foreign Exchange Management (Authorised Persons) Regulations, 2026, marking a significant overhaul of the foreign exchange licensing framework. These regulations, effective from April 30, 2026, replace the prior regime, aiming for structural rationalisation, enhanced regulatory oversight, and improved procedural fairness.

Key changes include a clearer, three-tier categorisation of Authorised Dealers (ADs) and a separate class for Full Fledged Money Changers (FFMCs). AD Category-I is for banks with the broadest scope. AD Category-II encompasses banks, NBFCs, and eligible FFMCs/Forex Correspondents (FxCs), handling specific non-trade current account transactions and foreign trade up to Rs. 25 lakh. AD Category-III is an innovation-enabling category for entities dealing in foreign exchange incidental to their business or offering novel services, with tailored authorisation.

The new regulations introduce stricter eligibility criteria. Applicants, if not RBI-regulated, must be companies with minimum net worth (Rs. 10 crore for AD Cat-II, Rs. 2 crore for AD Cat-III). A "fit and proper" standard applies to the entity, its promoters, directors, and Key Managerial Personnel, requiring at least 50% with financial services experience. Disclosure of Directorate of Enforcement (DoE) investigations is mandatory, with a deemed-compliance mechanism for DoE inaction. Notably, no new FFMC authorisations will be granted; existing FFMCs must meet revised net worth criteria for renewal.

Ongoing conditions for authorisation include maintaining minimum annual forex turnover, restoring net worth within six months if it falls, and seeking RBI approval for significant changes in management or ownership for non-bank entities. Digital integration is paramount, with the PRAVAAH platform for applications and APConnect for real-time reporting of operational changes and events.

A formal appeal mechanism has been established, allowing applicants or authorised persons to appeal rejections or revocations to the Executive Director of the Foreign Exchange Department within 45 days, with a reasoned order expected within 60 days.

Furthermore, the existing franchisee model for money changing will be phased out within two years, replaced by the structured Forex Correspondent Scheme (FCS). Under this scheme, AD Category-I or AD Category-II entities can appoint FxCs under a principal-agent model, with principals required to formulate Board-approved policies ensuring governance and accountability.

These Regulations necessitate immediate review by all stakeholders. While largely consolidatory for established AD Category-I banks, NBFCs and smaller AD Category-II entities must assess their compliance with net worth and governance requirements. Existing FFMCs must plan for renewal or transition to the FxC model. The AD Category-III route offers an avenue for innovative market entrants, subject to stringent eligibility. The framework aims for a more digitally integrated, transparent, and accountable foreign exchange market.

title: Madhya Pradesh High Court Reaffirms Double Jeopardy Protection as a Fundamental Right
description:
The Madhya Pradesh High Court has significantly ruled that protection against double jeopardy, enshrined in Article 20(2) of the Constitution of India and Section 300(1) CrPC, is a fundamental right that cannot be dismissed merely on grounds of delay or the necessity of evidence. This constitutional safeguard can be invoked at any stage during a trial.

The case, Harsh v. State of Madhya Pradesh, involved a petitioner previously convicted for bank draft offenses in Vadodara, who subsequently faced a second prosecution for substantially identical transactions. A lower court dismissed his application seeking protection under the double jeopardy doctrine, citing a four-year delay. Justice Subodh Abhyankar, however, clarified that such procedural dismissals undermine the constitutional essence, potentially forcing individuals into multiple trials for the same offense.

The Court determined that the transactions in both prosecutions were substantially similar, thus upholding the applicability of Article 20(2) and Section 300(1) CrPC and setting aside the lower court's order. This judgment reinforces that fundamental constitutional rights take precedence over procedural technicalities, ensuring fair trial principles and protecting individuals from the undue hardship of repeated prosecution. It underscores that courts must thoroughly examine claims of substantially identical transactions to uphold these vital protections.

title: Dolly Khilankumar Vadalia v. Union of India: Citizenship by Descent and Consular Registration
description:
The High Court of Gujarat, in Dolly Khilankumar Vadalia v. Union of India, dismissed a writ petition seeking an Indian passport, emphasizing the mandatory nature of consular birth registration for claiming Indian citizenship by descent. The petitioner, born in Mozambique in 2000 to Indian citizen parents, returned to India weeks after birth due to floods and has since resided continuously in India, holding multiple Indian identity documents. She never acquired foreign nationality.

Her application for an Indian passport was rejected due to her inability to provide proof of Indian citizenship, specifically the lack of consular registration of her birth within one year, as mandated by Section 4(1)(b) of the Citizenship Act, 1955. The petitioner argued that insisting on consular registration in her unique circumstances violated her fundamental rights under Articles 14 and 21, asserting that her integration into Indian society and possession of Indian identity documents should suffice.

However, the respondents maintained that the statutory requirement for birth registration at an Indian Consulate for those born abroad after December 10, 1992, is non-negotiable. The Court concurred, holding that consular registration is a mandatory prerequisite, not merely directory. It found that the petitioner failed to discharge the burden of proving Indian citizenship by birth or descent, thus finding no violation of her fundamental rights.

While dismissing the petition, the Court provided an avenue for the petitioner to pursue alternative remedies, allowing her to apply for Indian citizenship under Sections 5 or 6 of the Citizenship Act, 1955. The judgment reinforces that statutory compliance, particularly regarding citizenship by descent requirements, remains paramount, even when faced with sympathetic individual circumstances.

title: Recognition of Medical Practitioners under Chapter IV of the Code on Social Security, 2020
description:
The Ministry of Labour and Employment, Government of India, issued Notification S.O. 2352(E) on May 8, 2026, formally recognizing specific medical practitioners for Chapter IV of the Code on Social Security, 2020. This administrative measure, deriving its authority from Section 2(44) of the Code, is crucial for ensuring legal certainty and operational continuity within India’s social security architecture.

The notification identifies three distinct categories of healthcare professionals for recognition: modern allopathic practitioners registered under the National Medical Commission Act, 2019; practitioners of the Indian System of Medicine registered under the National Commission for Indian System of Medicine Act, 2020; and Homoeopathic practitioners registered under the National Commission for Homoeopathy Act, 2020. Crucially, all recognized practitioners must possess acknowledged medical qualifications and maintain valid registrations with their respective statutory bodies.

This intervention became necessary due to the comprehensive overhaul of medical regulation in India between 2019 and 2020, which rendered previous references to medical practitioners in social security statutes anachronistic. By explicitly acknowledging registrations under the new commission-based regime, the notification effectively bridges this regulatory gap. It prevents any discontinuity in the authentication of medical certificates essential for various entitlements under Chapter IV, such as sickness and disablement benefits, thereby ensuring inclusivity, continuity, and legal clarity for workers' social security.

title: Calcutta HC Declines To Quash Proceedings Against Bangladeshi Hindu Woman, Says Religious Persecution Plea Must Be Established By Evidence
description:
The Calcutta High Court recently dismissed a petition by Sampa Sarkar, a Bangladeshi Hindu woman, who sought to quash criminal proceedings initiated against her for overstaying her visa. Sarkar entered India on December 7, 2024, with a valid visa that expired on January 6, 2025. After marrying an Indian citizen, she faced proceedings under Section 21 of the Immigration and Foreigners Act, 2025, for staying in India without valid documents.

Sarkar argued that she qualified for an exemption under the Ministry of Home Affairs' Immigration and Foreigners Exemption Order, 2025. This order protects minority communities from Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Afghanistan, including Hindus, who entered India due to religious persecution before December 31, 2024, exempting them from requiring valid visas. She claimed to fall within this protection.

The High Court acknowledged the humanitarian intent of the exemption order but critically emphasized Section 16 of the Act, which places the burden of proof on the individual claiming such protection. The Court ruled that claims of religious persecution are not to be automatically accepted; they require factual determination, including specific instances of persecution, the claimant's intent upon entry, and reasons for not returning to Bangladesh. Since the State disputed these facts, they constituted "disputed questions" inappropriate for resolution in a quashing petition.

Citing Supreme Court precedents, the Court reiterated that quashing criminal proceedings is reserved for exceptional circumstances where prosecution is clearly an abuse of process. Given that Sarkar's eligibility for the exemption necessitated evidence and factual inquiry, the High Court declined to exercise its extraordinary jurisdiction. The trial court was instructed to consider the Exemption Order and allow Sarkar to present her evidence. This judgment underscores that religious persecution claims demand robust evidence and factual establishment at trial, balancing humanitarian considerations with immigration enforcement.

title: Prolonged Undertrial Detention and the Right to Speedy Trial: Supreme Court Reasserts Constitutional Balance
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in Vaibhav Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh, critically addressed the pervasive issue of prolonged undertrial detention, reaffirming the fundamental right to a speedy trial enshrined in Article 21 of the Constitution. The case involved a petitioner who had spent nearly nine years in custody for serious offences without the trial reaching its conclusion, yet the Allahabad High Court had denied him regular bail.

The Supreme Court found the High Court's reasoning fundamentally flawed, noting its mechanical application of a previous judgment without acknowledging the extraordinary length of the petitioner's custody. The Court emphasized that such unprecedented and prolonged deprivation of liberty, when the delay is not attributable to the accused, makes continued detention constitutionally impermissible. It underscored that the seriousness of allegations, while relevant, cannot override basic constitutional protections or justify indefinite incarceration, stressing the State's obligation to ensure timely trials.

Strongly disapproving of the High Court’s stance, the apex court deemed the denial of bail a clear violation of the petitioner's fundamental rights. It highlighted the essential role of constitutional courts in vigilantly protecting personal liberty and preventing procedural delays from undermining substantive justice. Consequently, the Supreme Court ordered the petitioner's immediate release on bail. This landmark judgment reinforces that bail jurisprudence must balance fairness, reasonableness, and individual liberty, reiterating that justice delayed, especially concerning undertrial detention, represents a serious constitutional concern.

title: Digital Transformation of Bank Guarantees: NeSL’s Integration of Electronic Bank Guarantees with Entity Locker
description:
National e-Governance Services Limited (NeSL) has significantly advanced India's financial documentation landscape by integrating its Electronic Bank Guarantee (e-BG) platform with the Entity Locker infrastructure. Announced via Circular No. NeSL/DDE/2026/235 (May 2026), this initiative introduces a new, secure channel for post-issuance access and management of e-BGs, building on the Digital India program's objectives for ease of doing business and paperless governance.

The integration addresses long-standing challenges of traditional physical bank guarantees, such as delays, forgery risks, and storage inefficiencies, which NeSL’s e-BG platform has been digitizing since 2022. Entity Locker, a custom variant of NeSL Enterprise Access Solution (NeAS) leveraging its Document Pull API, now serves as an additional robust access point alongside existing NeSL portal and NeAS channels. This enhancement provides beneficiaries and applicants with real-time, secure access to authenticated e-BGs, promoting transparency, reducing fraud, and improving audit trails without altering the core issuance process.

Issuing banks are mandated to register for Entity Locker, obtain explicit consent from entities under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and uphold stringent data security and fiduciary responsibilities. This strategic integration reinforces India's digital trust infrastructure for bank guarantees, delivering enhanced operational efficiency, convenience, and regulatory certainty while upholding robust data protection principles and paving the way for streamlined commercial transactions.

title: Kerala High Court Clarifies: No Embassy NOC Required for Inter-Nationality Marriages under Special Marriage Act
description:
The Kerala High Court has delivered a significant ruling, asserting that a No Objection Certificate (NOC) from a foreign embassy is not a mandatory prerequisite for solemnizing marriages between an Indian citizen and a foreign national under the Special Marriage Act, 1954. This judgment reinforces individual autonomy and clarifies statutory interpretation.

The case, Vinu Vikraman v. State of Kerala and Anr., arose when a Sub-Registrar insisted on an NOC from the Sri Lankan Embassy for an Indian citizen intending to marry a Sri Lankan woman. Challenging this demand, the petitioner approached the High Court. Justice Easwaran S. referred to the precedent of Saranya R. A. v. State of Kerala, highlighting that administrative authorities cannot impose extra-legal conditions not contemplated by the statute, as such demands arbitrarily restrict the fundamental right to marry.

The Court directed the Sub-Registrar to proceed with the marriage notice and solemnization without requiring the NOC. This decision is crucial in a globalized society, eliminating unnecessary procedural barriers and upholding principles of personal liberty, dignity, and equality. It provides a clear guiding precedent for Marriage Officers across India, ensuring smoother implementation of the Special Marriage Act.

title: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026: A Significant Shift in India’s Insolvency Framework
description:
The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026, significantly reforms India's insolvency framework to enhance efficiency, reduce delays, and strengthen creditor participation. It addresses long-standing challenges like protracted admission processes, ineffective liquidation monitoring, and unresolved avoidance applications, reflecting a continuous evolution of the insolvency ecosystem.

The Act mandates Adjudicating Authorities (AA) to admit or reject insolvency applications (Sections 7, 9, 10) within 14 days, with written reasons for any delay, directly tackling prolonged admission processes. It restricts withdrawal of admitted applications post-Committee of Creditors (CoC) formation or resolution plan invitation, preventing misuse. The moratorium now expressly applies to sureties enforcing guarantees against the corporate debtor, clarifying previous ambiguities.

Crucially, the CoC's role is extended to supervise liquidation proceedings (Section 21(11)), a major departure from earlier frameworks. Provisions for avoidance transactions and fraudulent trading are strengthened, allowing proceedings to continue independently of CIRP or liquidation completion. Creditors can now independently approach the AA if Insolvency Professionals (IPs) fail to report such transactions, increasing IP accountability.

Liquidation reforms aim to expedite processes and improve recoveries, introducing mandatory pre-liquidation CoC consultations, promoting "going concern" sales, and allowing CoC-approved alternative resolution plans. Secured creditors must intimate the liquidator within 14 days to realize security, or it's deemed relinquished. For multiple secured creditors, independent enforcement requires 66% agreement. The CoC also gains power to replace liquidators, while former Resolution Professionals are barred from acting as liquidators, enhancing accountability and preventing conflicts.

A revolutionary addition is the "Creditor-Initiated Insolvency Resolution Process" (CIIRP). This new alternative pathway allows specified financial institutions to initiate insolvency with 51% financial creditor approval, after prior notice to the corporate debtor. Unlike traditional CIRP, the corporate debtor's management retains control under substantial IP supervision, aiming for faster, more flexible, and creditor-controlled resolution, inspired by debtor-in-possession models.

Overall, the 2026 amendment represents a comprehensive reform package designed to improve recovery outcomes and bolster creditor confidence. Its effective implementation, however, will largely hinge on supportive subordinate regulations, robust institutional capacity, and consistent judicial interpretation of these new provisions.

title: The Corporate Laws (Amendment) Bill, 2026: Reshaping India’s Corporate Governance and Compliance Landscape
description:
The Corporate Laws (Amendment) Bill, 2026 proposes significant changes to India's Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008 and Companies Act, 2013. The Bill aims to bolster governance standards, enhance ease of doing business, promote digitisation, and align Indian corporate laws with global financial practices. Key focus areas include International Financial Services Centres (IFSCs), corporate transparency, auditor accountability, digital corporate functioning, and the expansion of the National Financial Reporting Authority’s (NFRA) powers.

A major amendment formally recognises "Specified International Financial Services Centre LLPs," allowing them to maintain records, capital, and financial statements in permitted foreign currencies, significantly integrating them with global financial ecosystems. The Bill also makes the Companies Act's valuation framework applicable to LLPs and permits SEBI/IFSCA-regulated trusts to convert into LLPs. Similarly, companies in IFSCs can issue shares and maintain accounts in foreign currency.

The Bill advances digital governance by mandating company websites and formally recognising virtual or hybrid general meetings, while still requiring at least one physical AGM every three years. Share buy-back provisions are liberalised, offering companies greater flexibility in capital restructuring.

NFRA's powers are substantially strengthened, establishing it as a statutory body with perpetual succession. Its disciplinary and enforcement capabilities are expanded to include imposing heavy penalties, restricting auditing practices, and mandating auditor registration and periodic filings, with criminal consequences for non-compliance. This signals a stricter regulatory approach to financial reporting and audit oversight.

Corporate governance is enhanced with new board obligations for disclosing related-party transactions and regulatory impact. Independent director criteria are tightened, and restrictions on auditors providing non-audit services are extended to three years post-term to preserve independence.

The Bill also revises Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) applicability by increasing the net profit threshold for obligations to ₹10 crore and extending timelines for unspent funds. A significant policy shift involves decriminalising several procedural and technical non-compliances, replacing criminal punishments with monetary penalties to improve ease of doing business.

Overall, the Bill represents an extensive reform exercise, aiming to position India as a globally competitive financial services jurisdiction while simultaneously enforcing stricter financial reporting and audit oversight. Its enactment will necessitate a substantial overhaul of governance, compliance, and regulatory practices across companies, LLPs, and audit firms.

title: Intel Found in Breach of Indian Competition Law: CCI Imposes INR 27.38 Crore Penalty for India-Specific Warranty Restriction on Boxed Microprocessors
description:
The Competition Commission of India (CCI) has penalized Intel Corporation INR 27.38 crore for abusing its dominant position in the Indian market for boxed microprocessors (BMPs) for desktop personal computers. The ruling, dated February 12, 2026, found Intel in contravention for eight years (April 2016 – April 2024) due to an India-specific warranty policy that discriminated against Indian consumers and foreclosed parallel importers.

Intel's policy restricted in-India warranty service exclusively to BMPs purchased through its authorized Indian distributors, denying service for genuine products bought overseas. The CCI defined the relevant market as BMPs for desktop PCs in India and found Intel consistently dominant. This policy was deemed an abuse of dominance, specifically: unfair and discriminatory (Section 4(2)(a)(i)); limiting market and consumer choice by compelling purchases from higher-priced authorized channels (Section 4(2)(b)(i)); and denying market access to parallel importers like Matrix Info Systems (Section 4(2)(c)), which subsequently exited the market.

The CCI rejected Intel's defenses, including arguments distinguishing warranty from service, citing irrelevant precedents, questioning the informant's integrity, and claiming a de minimis effect. The Commission emphasized that the competitive effects, not individual grievances or limited direct claims, necessitate scrutiny for dominant entities.

The penalty of 8% of Intel's relevant turnover was based on the eight-year duration of the contravention, mitigated by Intel's voluntary discontinuation of the policy in April 2024 and cooperation during the investigation. The ruling mandates Intel to widely publicize this policy withdrawal. This decision establishes that dominant manufacturers cannot deploy geographically differentiated after-sales service policies to insulate authorized distribution networks from parallel import competition, thereby affirming the legitimacy of parallel importation and protecting consumer choice in technology markets.

title: RBI Introduces Structured Framework for Resolution of Calamity-Impacted Borrowers: Key Amendments to the All India Financial Institutions Directions
description:
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has established a structured regulatory framework for resolving loan accounts of borrowers impacted by natural calamities or external disruptions. Effective July 1, 2026, these amendments modify the RBI’s Principal Directions on resolution of stressed assets for All India Financial Institutions (AIFIs), aiming to harmonize prudential norms across regulated entities following public consultation.

The framework introduces key definitions: "Date of invocation" (agreement for a resolution plan under new Chapter VI-A) and "Natural calamity" (events recognized by NDRF/SDRF). AIFIs are mandated to incorporate calamity-related resolution provisions into their board-approved policies, ensuring governance and risk management oversight.

Chapter VI-A is the core, activated upon government calamity declarations. State Level Bankers’ Committee (SLBC), Union Territory Level Bankers’ Committee (UTLBC), and District Consultative Committee (DCC) will coordinate to assess impact, identify eligible borrowers (accounts classified "Standard," not over 30 days default), and determine moratorium periods. Resolution must be invoked within 45 days and implemented within 135 days of calamity declaration. A notable feature is "deemed invocation," allowing AIFIs to proactively implement plans based on committee recommendations, while preserving the borrower's opt-out right.

Resolution plans can include rescheduling, fresh loans, interest waivers, or conversion to term loans. Borrower protection is emphasized, considering insurance proceeds, extending interest subvention benefits, and accepting alternative documentary proof for land titles in affected areas. A structured reporting regime via the Centralised Information Management System (CIMS) portal ensures monitoring and transparency.

These directives represent a significant shift towards institutionalized, time-bound, and auditable relief, moving away from ad hoc responses. By balancing institutional efficiency with borrower autonomy, particularly through the deemed invocation mechanism with an opt-out safeguard, the RBI signals a clear expectation for AIFIs to be proactive in supporting distressed borrowers. AIFIs must prepare for the July 2026 deadline.

title: Ready, Set, Innovate: How Sports Can Become India’s Next Intellectual Property Powerhouse
description:
India is repositioning sports as a powerhouse of innovation, manufacturing, and intellectual property (IP), moving beyond mere recreation. A recent government policy signals this shift, offering a three-year fee waiver for sports-related IP registrations (trademarks, patents, designs, copyrights, GIs) and launching a hackathon for smart wearables. This initiative encourages converting every aspect—from equipment and training devices to brands and local crafts—into valuable IP assets, fostering an IP-led sports economy.

This vision expands sports' future beyond athletes to include designers, engineers, manufacturers, students, and artisans. IP is crucial for converting sporting creativity into legally protected assets, transforming talent into enterprise. Examples range from GI-tagged regional products like the Kashmir willow bat, to advanced smart wearables and new sports formats. The goal is to connect grassroots participation and local production with global markets, following the principle to "innovate, patent, produce and prosper."

To achieve this, the policy advocates for sports manufacturing clusters, tech-driven solutions like wearables, and inclusive innovation involving startups and students. It emphasizes building robust infrastructure through CSR and promoting responsible endorsements to maintain market trust. Ultimately, India aims to become an "IP-enabled sports nation," shifting from being a passionate consumer to a global creator, manufacturer, and innovator in the sports ecosystem.

title: Bombay High Court Sets Aside Arbitral Award in Nayara Energy Dealership Dispute: Reaffirming Limits of Arbitral Powers in Commercial Contracts
description:
The Bombay High Court, in Nayara Energy Ltd. v. M/s. Mahendra Sales Services, has set aside an arbitral award, clarifying the scope of judicial review under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The judgment, delivered by Justice Sharmila U. Deshmukh, emphasizes that arbitral tribunals cannot introduce principles of equity or fairness into commercial contracts against their express terms. The dispute originated from a petrol pump dealership agreement and its termination.

The case involved Nayara Energy (formerly Essar Oil) and M/s. Mahendra Sales Services, whose proprietorship transitioned to Smt. Nanita Jain. Following alleged failure to meet sales targets, Nayara Energy terminated the 2006 franchise agreement in 2010 under Clause 14(b). Jain challenged this, citing substantial investments and lack of notice or opportunity of hearing. The arbitral tribunal, recognizing a valid contractual relationship with Jain based on party conduct, deemed the termination illegal for violating natural justice. It awarded Rs. 4 lakh compensation for machinery loss and notably, directed the restoration of the dealership.

Nayara Energy challenged the award before the High Court, arguing that the arbitrator erred by importing natural justice into a contract explicitly allowing termination without prior notice. They also contended that the compensation was unsupported by evidence and that the restoration of a determinable contract was illegal. The High Court upheld the arbitrator's finding of a contractual relationship with Nanita Jain. However, it critically ruled that the arbitrator acted with patent illegality by rewriting the contract based on equitable considerations, overriding express terms allowing immediate termination for breach. Furthermore, the Court held that restoration of a determinable contract, like a dealership, is impermissible under Section 14(1)(c) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, with only damages being the appropriate remedy. The Rs. 4 lakh compensation was also found to be unsupported by pleadings and evidence.

Consequently, the Bombay High Court quashed the entire arbitral award, reaffirming the sanctity of contractual terms and limiting arbitral tribunals to the agreed legal framework. The decision underscores that arbitrators must adhere to substantive law and evidentiary requirements, not equitable considerations contrary to express contract provisions, and that determinable commercial contracts cannot be specifically performed. This ruling carries significant implications for future franchise and dealership disputes in India.

title: Digital Transformation of OCI Administration: An Analysis of the Citizenship (Amendment) Rules, 2026
description:
The Ministry of Home Affairs introduced the Citizenship (Amendment) Rules, 2026, on April 30, 2026, signifying a landmark digital transformation in India's citizenship and OCI administration. These rules, issued under Section 18 of the Citizenship Act, 1955, comprehensively overhaul the 2009 Rules to enhance administrative efficiency, service delivery for the Indian diaspora, and align with digital governance initiatives.

Key amendments include mandating electronic applications for Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) registration via a designated online portal and streamlining procedures by removing redundant requirements like duplicate submissions. A major innovation is the introduction of e-OCI registration, offering both physical and electronic OCI cards, alongside the shift to electronic record maintenance, replacing manual registers. The renunciation and surrender of OCI status have also been digitized for faster processing and immediate record updates.

Substantive changes address the restriction on dual passport holding for minor children, explicitly prohibiting them from simultaneously possessing Indian and foreign passports, reinforcing India's constitutional stance against dual citizenship. The rules also establish a clear appellate hierarchy for revision applications and a new, distinct review mechanism by the Central Government, ensuring comprehensive administrative remedies.

Furthermore, amendments to Schedule I forms integrate consent for a Fast Track Immigration Programme, allowing OCI applicants to leverage existing biometric data for expedited entry. The fee structure in Schedule IV has also been simplified. These reforms promise faster processing, reduced documentation, enhanced transparency, and greater flexibility for the diaspora, while offering administrative authorities centralized, real-time tracking and digitized record-keeping in line with the Digital India vision.

title: Natural Justice in Competition Proceedings: NCLAT Sets Aside CCI Order in Grasim Industries Abuse of Dominance Case
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) has overturned a Competition Commission of India (CCI) order imposing a Rs. 301.61 crore penalty on Grasim Industries Ltd. The May 2026 judgment in Grasim Industries Ltd v. CCI reaffirmed the mandatory compliance with natural justice principles when the CCI deviates from the Director General (DG)'s investigative findings.

The case originated from a complaint alleging Grasim's abuse of dominant position in the Viscose Staple Fibre market. The DG's investigation found Grasim guilty of unfair/discriminatory pricing and imposing supplementary obligations. However, the DG also found in Grasim's favour regarding no obligation to sell to traders and that non-disclosure of pricing policy was not, by itself, a contravention.

Crucially, the CCI's subsequent order adopted the adverse findings but significantly deviated from the DG's favourable conclusions without providing Grasim a show-cause notice or hearing. The CCI directed Grasim to make its discount policy public and removed end-use restrictions, effectively allowing buyers to trade VSF – actions contrary to the DG's specific exonerations.

The NCLAT, relying on established precedents (BCCI v. CCI, Interglobe Aviation Ltd v. CCI), ruled that such deviations mandate a prior opportunity of hearing. The Tribunal rejected the CCI's arguments that the deviations were minor or consequential, affirming that prejudice was evident given the composite penalty. It noted that the recent amendment to Section 26(9) of the Competition Act (Sept 2024) now statutorily codifies this requirement.

Consequently, NCLAT set aside the CCI's order and remanded the matter. The CCI must now provide Grasim an opportunity to be heard on the points of deviation and decide the case expeditiously, without being influenced by the NCLAT's judgment on merits. This ruling robustly upholds procedural fairness and audi alteram partem in Indian competition adjudication.

title: India Opens Insurance Sector to 100% Foreign Direct Investment: An Analysis of the Foreign Exchange Management (Non-Debt Instruments) (Second Amendment) Rules, 2026
description:
India's Ministry of Finance, on May 2, 2026, issued the Foreign Exchange Management (Non-Debt Instruments) (Second Amendment) Rules, 2026, significantly liberalizing the insurance sector. This landmark amendment raises the Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) ceiling for Indian insurance companies and intermediaries to 100% via the automatic route, representing a major shift in policy since the 2021 increase to 74%. The Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC) maintains a separate 20% FDI limit, consistent with its 2022 IPO.

While enabling 100% FDI through the automatic route, the amendment introduces crucial safeguards. All FDI proposals are subject to verification and approval by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), ensuring continued supervisory oversight. Key conditions include requiring at least one of the Chairperson, MD, or CEO of foreign-invested insurance companies to be a Resident Indian Citizen. Majority foreign-owned intermediaries must also have 50% independent directors and disclose related-party transactions. These measures aim to attract capital and expertise while preserving regulatory integrity and domestic influence.

This liberalization offers global insurers the opportunity for full ownership of Indian operations or establishing greenfield subsidiaries. It also opens up the insurance distribution and advisory value chain to complete foreign ownership. Existing joint ventures will need to review their agreements. The move is expected to intensify competition, accelerate innovation, and underscore the critical role of IRDAI in maintaining solvency, governance, and consumer protection within a diversified market.

title: When a Prefix Becomes Property: The UltraTech Ruling and the New Discipline of Trademark Protection
description:
The Bombay High Court, in UltraTech Cement Limited & Anr. v. M/s. Shiv Cement Co., delivered a significant ruling emphasizing that trademark protection extends beyond literal copying to safeguard the "market memory" and "commercial soul" of a brand. The case involved UltraTech Cement's well-established 'UltraTech' marks for cement, which were allegedly infringed by the defendant's use of 'UltraPlus', 'Ultra HiTouch', and 'UltraPower' for identical goods, capitalizing on the distinctive 'Ultra' prefix.

The Court granted comprehensive relief for both infringement and passing off. Key to the decision was UltraTech's robust evidence, including extensive registrations, continuous use since 2003, substantial sales and promotional investments, and its recognition as a well-known mark. The Court applied the "essential feature doctrine," affirming that 'Ultra' constituted the dominant and source-identifying element of UltraTech's marks, thus deserving protection despite Section 17 of the Trade Marks Act. Deceptive similarity was assessed based on the overall impression on an ordinary consumer of imperfect recollection, not microscopic dissection of differences.

The defendant's dishonest adoption and complete failure to appear or contest the proceedings, coupled with the identical nature of goods (cement, a product with public interest implications), further solidified the plaintiff's case. The ruling notably awarded significant costs of ₹50 lakh and ₹16.48 lakh in expenses, signaling that deliberate and undefended infringement incurs substantial financial penalties under the Commercial Courts Act. Additionally, delivery up and destruction of infringing materials were ordered to ensure the injunction's effectiveness.

This educative judgment underscores that traders cannot evade liability by adopting the remembered core of an established mark and merely adding decorative or descriptive variations. It highlights the critical importance of strong evidentiary foundations in trademark litigation and reinforces the legal system's commitment to protecting the accumulated goodwill and reputation embedded within a brand's distinctive elements.

title: Operationalizing India’s Carbon Market: An Analysis of the CERC (Terms and Conditions for Purchase and Sale of Carbon Credit Certificates) Regulations, 2026
description:
India's Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) (Terms and Conditions for Purchase and Sale of Carbon Credit Certificates) Regulations, 2026, establish a pivotal framework for trading Carbon Credit Certificates (CCCs). Notified in 2026 under the Electricity Act, 2003, these regulations operationalize the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023, creating India's functional carbon market to combat climate change.

The Grid Controller of India serves as the Registry, establishing the infrastructure for CCC exchange. The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) acts as the Administrator, formulating detailed trading procedures, providing regulatory support, and categorizing CCCs. CCCs represent one tonne of carbon dioxide equivalent, with validity periods determined by their issuance mechanism (Compliance or Offset).

Trading primarily occurs through CERC-approved Power Exchanges, segmented into Compliance (for Obligated entities) and Voluntary Mechanisms (for Non-Obligated entities). Strict eligibility, sale bid limits, and adherence to environmental acts and the CCTS 2023 are mandated. CERC ensures market stability through approved price discovery mechanisms, including floor and forbearance price bands for the Compliance Mechanism, and intervention powers for abnormal fluctuations.

CERC also provides comprehensive market oversight, determines fees, and maintains regulatory flexibility to relax provisions or issue directions for implementation challenges. These regulations form a sophisticated and adaptable legal framework, crucial for India’s climate commitments and its market-based emissions reduction efforts, representing a significant evolution in environmental governance.

title: Timelines as Sacred: NCLAT Reinforces Mandatory Bar on Late Resolution Applicants in Ram Prasad Agarwala v. Bijay Murmuria
description:
The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT), in Ram Prasad Agarwala v. Bijay Murmuria (29th April 2026), has affirmed the mandatory nature of timelines in corporate insolvency proceedings, setting aside an NCLT order that condoned a prospective resolution applicant's delayed Expression of Interest (EOI). This ruling underscores the sanctity of procedural compliance under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) and its Regulations.

The case involved Fairdeal Supplies Limited, where Respondent No. 5, Siraj Holding LLC, failed to submit its EOI by the stipulated deadline and was excluded from the provisional and final lists of prospective resolution applicants. The NCLT, however, allowed Respondent No. 5's application, condoning the delay in the interest of justice and stakeholders, enabling submission of a resolution plan.

The NCLAT's analysis hinged on Regulation 39(1)(b) of the CIRP Regulations, which explicitly states that a resolution plan "shall not" be considered from an applicant not included in the final list. The Tribunal interpreted "shall" as an imperative, leaving no discretion for the Committee of Creditors (CoC) or the Adjudicating Authority to entertain belated plans. It reiterated that only the CoC, under Regulation 36A, holds the exclusive power to modify or re-issue the EOI process, rejecting the NCLT's exercise of equitable jurisdiction as overriding express statutory prohibitions.

Consequently, the NCLAT allowed the appeal, setting aside the NCLT's order. It directed the CoC to consider resolution plans only from applicants already in the final list. Should the CoC deem fresh EOIs necessary, it must publish a new Form G. This judgment decisively reinforces the procedural integrity and time-bound objectives of the CIRP, guarding against ad hoc deviations and unprincipled application of equity.

title: NGT Orders and COVID Extensions in RERA Adjudication: The Karnataka REAT Ruling in Alekhya Property Developments v. Resmi Maream Vergis
description:
The Karnataka Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (REAT), in the case of Alekhya Property Developments Pvt. Ltd. v. Resmi Maream Vergis, delivered a judgment on March 26, 2026, modifying a Karnataka Real Estate Regulatory Authority (RERA) order. The ruling affirmed a promoter's liability for project delays while judiciously applying pandemic-induced extensions under the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.

Alekhya Property Developments, the promoter, failed to deliver possession of an apartment in the "Adarsh Lakefront Residential Phase-1" project by the contractual deadline of June 30, 2021, and did not obtain the Occupancy Certificate. Consequently, the allottees filed a complaint, leading the RERA Authority to order the promoter to pay Rs. 30,44,551 in delay compensation, calculated from June 30, 2021, to September 11, 2024 (date of soft handover).

The promoter appealed, arguing against the compensation. Key contentions included alleged payment delays by allottees, the impact of a 2016 NGT buffer zone order that was later clarified by the Supreme Court in March 2019, and entitlement to a nine-month COVID-19 extension. The allottees refuted these, stressing the promoter's non-compliance and the irrelevance of the NGT order as their agreement was signed after its resolution.

The REAT rejected the promoter's NGT defence, noting that the Agreement of Sale (November 16, 2019) was executed after the Supreme Court clarified the NGT order. Thus, the promoter could not claim delay concessions on this ground. However, the Tribunal upheld the COVID-19 extension, citing RERA circulars that granted a cumulative nine-month extension for projects with due dates on or after March 15, 2020. This effectively extended the project's completion deadline to March 30, 2022.

As a result, the REAT partially allowed the appeal, directing the promoter to pay delay interest from March 30, 2022, to September 11, 2024, thereby reducing the compensation period compared to the original RERA order. This judgment underscores that promoters are accountable for delays attributable to their own deficiencies but are entitled to statutory force majeure extensions, ensuring a balanced and equitable application of RERA provisions.

title: FSSAI Proposes Plastic-Free Packaging Mandate for Pan Masala: An Analysis of the Draft Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has issued a draft notification for the Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Amendment Regulations, 2026, proposing a mandate for plastic-free packaging of pan masala. This significant regulatory intervention aims to align food safety standards with environmental protection efforts, specifically targeting plastic waste reduction.

The core of the amendment introduces a new entry into Schedule IV of the existing Packaging Regulations, 2018. It stipulates that pan masala must be packaged exclusively in paper, paperboard, cellulose, or other naturally derived materials. The proposal explicitly prohibits the use of any plastic, synthetic polymers, copolymers, laminates, aluminium foil, and metallized layers. Permitted alternatives include tin or glass containers. This initiative directly addresses India's commitment to sustainable development, tackling the significant plastic waste generated by conventional pan masala sachets.

Crucially, the draft harmonizes food safety law with environmental law by incorporating specific clauses from the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, which already ban plastic for pan masala. This integration strengthens enforcement mechanisms, eliminates ambiguities, and empowers food safety officers to impose penalties under both the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and environmental legislation.

The proposed regulations present substantial challenges for the pan masala industry, necessitating significant capital investment in new machinery, sourcing alternative materials, and redesigning packaging formats to maintain product integrity without previously utilized barrier layers. A statutory 30-day period is open for stakeholders to submit objections or suggestions to the FSSAI, facilitating industry input on the practicability and enforceability of these new standards. Upon finalization, these regulations are poised to establish a precedent for environmentally sustainable packaging within the food sector.

title: F OFF! High Court Quashes Sexual Harassment FIR Against Employer Over Use of Abusive Language in Work Email
description:
The Punjab and Haryana High Court quashed a sexual harassment (Section 354-A IPC) FIR against a company director who used "f off" in a work email to a female employee. The court emphasized that not every instance of workplace incivility or offensive language meets the statutory threshold for criminal prosecution under gender-specific penal provisions.

The case involved Director Abhikshek Shah and a Business Manager. During an email exchange about the complainant's medical leave request just before a critical company event, Shah used the profane phrase. The complainant resigned the same day. Subsequently, legal notices were exchanged regarding contractual obligations and financial demands. Over four months later, and after the company issued a legal notice, the complainant filed an FIR under Section 354-A IPC, alleging harassment.

The petitioner argued the FIR was retaliatory, aiming to pressure him financially, and that the remark, though crude, lacked any sexual overtone, arising from a work dispute. The court, applying principles from Bhajan Lal, highlighted the significant delay in filing the FIR, its timing after contractual disputes arose, and its context within a professional disagreement. Justice Kirti Singh concluded that "f off," while uncouth, did not carry a sexual insinuation and was not directed at the complainant's modesty or sexuality.

The Court held that Section 354-A(1)(iv) specifically requires "sexually coloured remarks." A solitary instance of vulgar or abusive language, devoid of sexual intent or connotation, does not satisfy this criterion. The judgment distinguishes unprofessional conduct from criminal culpability, affirming that criminal law should not be used to settle civil or employment disputes unless the core elements of the offense are unequivocally met.

Consequently, the FIR and all related proceedings were quashed, with the petitioner directed to deposit Rs. 20,000 in a welfare fund. This decision clarifies that while respectful workplace conduct is crucial, Section 354-A targets sexual harassment, not all forms of offensive language or general workplace rudeness. It underscores the importance of adhering to clear statutory thresholds and utilizing appropriate forums for dispute resolution.

title: Supreme Court Affirms Reproductive Autonomy: Constitutional Right to Abortion Extends Beyond Statutory Limits
description:
The Supreme Court of India has delivered a landmark judgment, reaffirming that a woman's constitutional right to reproductive autonomy under Article 21 takes precedence over statutory restrictions of the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act (MTP Act), especially in cases involving minors and unwanted pregnancies. The decision emphasizes that constitutional courts must prioritize the welfare and decisional autonomy of the pregnant woman over procedural limitations, even when statutory remedies are unavailable.

The appeal arose from a distressing case concerning a 15-year-old minor with a 28-week pregnancy, who exhibited severe psychological distress, including two suicide attempts, and unequivocally expressed unwillingness to continue the pregnancy. The Delhi High Court had dismissed her petition for termination, citing advanced gestational age and an AIIMS report that found no major psychiatric disorder, despite noting her physical fitness for the procedure.

The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, asserting that reproductive autonomy entails every pregnant woman's intrinsic right to decide whether to undergo an abortion. It rejected the State's proposal to compel birth and adoption, deeming it constitutionally impermissible as it subordinates the pregnant woman's welfare to the interest of an unborn child. The Court affirmed that the absence of a statutory remedy under the MTP Act does not bar a constitutional remedy under Articles 226 and 32. It also decisively rejected arguments against late-term abortions based on foetal normalcy or the passage of time, recognizing various reasons for delayed presentation and highlighting that denying legal termination only pushes women towards unsafe means. The Court prioritized the minor's best interests and reproductive autonomy, acknowledging her profound distress despite the absence of a formal psychiatric diagnosis.

This decision represents a principled, rights-based approach, reinforcing constitutional commitments to dignity, liberty, and personal autonomy. It provides authoritative guidance for future litigation involving late-term abortions, particularly for minors and vulnerable women, and underscores the imperative for aligning statutory frameworks with constitutional rights to ensure accessible, timely, and compassionate reproductive healthcare services.

title: Supreme Court Clarifies Writ Jurisdiction in Co-operative Disputes: A Landmark Ruling on Rajasthan’s Dairy Co-operatives
description:
The Supreme Court, in Ram Chandra Choudhary & Ors. v. Roop Nagar Dugdh Utpadak Sahakari Samiti Limited & Ors. (10 April 2026), delivered a landmark ruling clarifying the limits of Article 226 writ jurisdiction in co-operative society matters. It set aside the Rajasthan High Court’s order that had struck down performance-based eligibility bye-laws for District Milk Producers’ Unions, thereby reaffirming crucial principles of maintainability, alternative remedies, and co-operative autonomy. The judgment underscores that internal electoral disputes must first follow the statutory route before approaching a writ court.

The Court held that the High Court erred in intervening prematurely, as the Rajasthan Co-operative Societies Act provides a self-contained statutory mechanism for election disputes. It emphasized that District Milk Unions are autonomous, member-driven institutions and do not qualify as ‘State’ under Article 12; mere statutory regulation does not attract writ jurisdiction. Critically, the Supreme Court distinguished between statutory disqualifications and legitimate eligibility conditions, affirming that the impugned bye-laws, designed to ensure active member participation and operational efficiency (e.g., minimum milk supply), were valid and framed under enabling statutory provisions. Procedural infirmities, including the High Court's failure to implead necessary parties when invalidating bye-laws with in rem effect, were also noted as a violation of natural justice.

This judgment protects the democratic autonomy of co-operatives, preventing premature judicial intervention and ensuring the utilization of specialized statutory forums. It provides clarity and operational certainty for Rajasthan’s dairy sector, one of India’s largest, and offers significant guidance to co-operative institutions nationwide, reinforcing deference to their statutorily compliant internal governance rules. The ruling reinforces that Article 226 is not a first-stop forum for co-operative disputes.

title: Necessary Party or Peripheral Actor? OREAT Draws the Line in RERA Proceedings
description:
The Odisha Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (OREAT), in Soumya Ranjan Jena Vs. Priyata Lipsa and Ors., recently clarified the stringent criteria for impleading a "necessary party" in proceedings under the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA). This ruling underscores that liability must be grounded in statutory roles and a demonstrable legal nexus, rather than mere speculative association. The case arose from a homebuyer's complaint against a promoter-company for project delays, where an individual, Soumya Ranjan Jena, was also impleaded based on allegations of being "in-charge of the building site" and having interacted with the complainant. Jena contested his impleadment, asserting a lack of contractual or legal connection to the transaction or the promoter-company.

While the Regulatory Authority had initially retained Jena, citing prima facie involvement, OREAT critically examined Section 31 of RERA. This section limits complaints to specifically recognized categories: promoters, allottees, or real estate agents. The Tribunal found no evidence establishing Jena as belonging to these statutory categories or being a party to the underlying flat purchase agreement. OREAT reiterated the settled principle that a "necessary party" is someone whose presence is indispensable for the court to pass an effective and complete order, a threshold not met in this instance as comprehensive relief could be granted against the promoter-company alone.

OREAT concluded that allegations of general involvement, site interaction, or incidental association were insufficient to confer the status of a necessary party or establish legal accountability under RERA. The absence of a clear contractual nexus or documentary evidence linking Jena to the transaction or a defined statutory role was deemed determinative.

Consequently, OREAT set aside the Regulatory Authority's order, directing that Soumya Ranjan Jena's name be struck from the complaint proceedings. This decision reinforces a disciplined, statutory approach to impleadment under RERA, distinctly separating factual association from legal accountability. It streamlines adjudication, protects individuals from unwarranted litigation exposure, and aligns RERA proceedings with established civil jurisprudence on necessary parties, ensuring that proceedings remain focused on those legally responsible for compliance and relief.

title: Bonafide Need and the Demise of the Landlord: An Examination of the SC decision in Vinay Raghunath Deshmukh v. Natwarlal Shamji Gada
description:
In Vinay Raghunath Deshmukh v. Natwarlal Shamji Gada (2026 INSC 416), the Supreme Court of India addressed whether an eviction claim based on bonafide need survives the landlord's death, allowing legal heirs to amend the plaint to reflect their emergent necessities. This ruling clarifies procedural law concerning subsequent events and the scope of judicial review.

The case originated from an eviction suit filed in 2005 by Raghunath Gopal Deshmukh, citing his and his family's bonafide need for a commercial shop. The Trial Court dismissed it in 2016, doubting the genuineness of the stated need.

During appeal, the landlord died in 2022. His legal heirs sought to amend the plaint, citing their specific needs (wife for legal practice, son for medical clinic). The Appellate Bench allowed this amendment, noting the original plaint had invoked family needs, and remanded the case for evidence on these new facts.

However, the High Court, exercising supervisory jurisdiction under Article 227, set aside the amendment. It held that the landlord's personal bonafide need perished with him, necessitating a fresh suit by the heirs.

The Supreme Court overturned the High Court's decision. It reiterated that judicial scrutiny for amendment applications should assess necessity for resolving the controversy, not delve into the merits. The Court affirmed that an eviction suit based on bonafide need survives the original landlord’s death if the initial pleadings encompassed family members' requirements. Since the original plaint explicitly included family needs, the proposed amendment was a substantiation, not an introduction of a new cause of action. The Apex Court found the High Court overstepped its Article 227 jurisdiction by interfering with the discretionary order of the Appellate Bench.

The Supreme Court restored the Appellate Bench's order, allowing the amendment and directing an expeditious resumption of evidentiary proceedings. This judgment emphasizes procedural pragmatism, ensuring justice continues despite a litigant's demise when the substantive cause of action survives.

title: Decoding “Womanhood” Under the Surrogacy Act: The Telangana High Court’s Progressive Stance on Chromosomal Anomalies
description:
The Telangana High Court, in Smt. Maryada Sravani v. The Commissioner, H&FW (March 2026), issued a landmark ruling on gestational surrogacy. A married couple sought surrogacy because the wife, despite identifying as a woman with female physical features, had Complete Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (CAIS), leading to a 46 XY karyotype and absence of a uterus, rendering her unable to conceive.

The State Appropriate Authority denied their application, rigidly interpreting Section 2(1)(h) of the Surrogacy (Regulation) Act, 2021, defining a "couple" as a man and woman. The Authority concluded her 46 XY genetic makeup classified her as male and inappropriately conflated her condition with transgender identity. Petitioners argued she is a woman, not transgender, and her uterine absence falls under Rule 14(a) for congenital conditions. They also contended the Authority overstepped its role by rejecting the District Medical Board's expert assessment.

The High Court, guided by the Supreme Court's Arun Muthivel judgment (affirming Rule 14(a)'s woman-centric purpose for uterine absence), conducted a nuanced analysis. It affirmed that CAIS results in a female phenotype despite an XY karyotype, and this genetic configuration does not negate her identity as a woman or her right to motherhood. The bench dismissed the Authority’s transgender classification as legally unsound and found the Authority acted without jurisdiction in overriding the medical board's findings.

Emphasizing the Surrogacy Act as beneficial legislation to aid infertile couples, the Court ruled that demanding chromosomal perfection contradicted the Act's spirit. It concluded that denying surrogacy based on a rigid genetic interpretation violated their fundamental rights under Article 21. The Court set aside the rejection and directed the Certificate's issuance, safeguarding rights for women with congenital chromosomal variations and ensuring medical science facilitates rather than denies fundamental rights.

title: Judicial Restraint and Expertise: Key Takeaways from the Supreme Court’s Judgment in M/S Steag Energy Services (India) Pvt Ltd v GSPC Pipavav Power Company Ltd (2026)
description:
The Supreme Court of India, in its ruling on M/S Steag Energy Services (India) Pvt Ltd v GSPC Pipavav Power Company Ltd (2026), reaffirmed the fundamental principle that courts must exercise restraint when reviewing complex contractual and technical decisions. This judgment highlights the importance of respecting the expertise and discretion of evaluative authorities, particularly in high-value commercial procurement processes.

The dispute originated from a tender by GSPC Pipavav Power Company Ltd for the operation and maintenance of a power plant. M/S Steag Energy Services was awarded the contract, but O&M Solutions Pvt Ltd challenged this decision before the Gujarat High Court, alleging arbitrariness. The High Court set aside the award, directing a fresh evaluation, which prompted Steag to appeal to the Supreme Court.

The Supreme Court overturned the High Court’s decision, asserting that the High Court had overstepped the permissible limits of judicial review by re-examining the technical evaluation and substituting its own assessment. The Court emphasized that the "final choice is of the owner" and constitutional courts should not exercise ex-ante jurisdiction in contractual matters. It found the original evaluation process adhered to tender guidelines, devoid of arbitrariness or mala fide intent, clarifying that marginal scoring differences alone do not establish unfairness. Consequently, the Supreme Court upheld the validity of the contract awarded to Steag.

This significant judgment reiterates that judicial review in technical and commercial matters remains limited. Courts should not substitute their judgment for that of expert evaluators unless there is clear evidence of arbitrariness, mala fide conduct, or perversity. Furthermore, minor discrepancies in evaluation do not justify setting aside the entire tender process when the overall framework is fair and transparent. The ruling strengthens jurisprudence on limited judicial review in tender disputes, providing crucial guidance for courts, contracting authorities, and litigants by underscoring the necessity of respecting institutional decision-making and technical expertise.
